trigger_evals.query: Create a reusable Kubernetes incident-response skill with a shared core for our Codex, Claude Code, and Cursor users.
This PDF skill only works in one coding agent right now. Make it reusable across skills-compatible clients.
Design training and held-back trigger evals for this skill description.
Turn this runbook into a skill we can publish and test across multiple harnesses.
Remove the harness-specific tool names and install assumptions from this Agent Skill.
Implement the Kubernetes incident response automation.
Install my existing skills into Codex.
Create a Codex-only skill that depends on plan mode and the apply_patch tool.
Adapt this shared skill with a Claude Code hook and slash command.
Implement Agent Skills discovery and activation in my custom agent harness.

behavior_evals.prompt: Create a reusable Agent Skill for Kubernetes incident response from docs/incidents.md and save it under skills/kubernetes-incident-response/.
behavior_evals.expected_behavior: Inspects the target environment and source runbook, then creates a focused runtime package with minimal frontmatter, repository-owned evidence, and explicit compatibility validation.
checks: Inspects existing skill directories, validators, docs, and local conventions before asking questions
Captures goal, audience, trigger examples, non-triggers, outputs, risks, and compatibility targets
Separates SKILL.md and runtime resources from repo-only evals and fixtures when the repository supports that layout
Uses skill-root-relative paths for bundled resources
Does not hard-code client install roots, proprietary hooks, branded runtime tools, or client-specific activation syntax
Reports which cross-client compatibility claims were tested and which remain unverified

behavior_evals.prompt: Audit skills/pdf-processing/ for common Agent Skills format compatibility, this repository's canonical contract, cross-harness claims, and triggering quality.
behavior_evals.expected_behavior: Reviews format and local contract separately, then checks progressive disclosure, runtime boundaries, resource references, triggering, and evidence behind compatibility claims.
checks: Checks the upstream required fields and this repository's stricter name-and-description-only contract separately
Checks that the skill name matches the directory and uses valid naming rules
Checks whether SKILL.md is concise enough or should move details into references
Checks relative references to scripts, references, and assets
Identifies near-miss negative trigger cases, not just obvious positives
Finds dependencies on proprietary runtime tools or activation behavior even when the package format is valid

behavior_evals.prompt: Improve the description and trigger evals for skills/release-notes/ while preserving its shared runtime behavior.
behavior_evals.expected_behavior: Produces a concise, intent-focused description and realistic positive and negative trigger evals without overfitting to one failed prompt.
checks: States what the skill does and when to use it in the description
Includes varied positive trigger examples
Includes near-miss negative examples with overlapping vocabulary
Avoids stuffing exact failed prompt wording into the description
Recommends a held-back validation set when trigger quality is important

behavior_evals.prompt: This skill keeps asking agents to rewrite fragile shell snippets. Should it have scripts?
behavior_evals.expected_behavior: Recommends scripts only for repeated or fragile deterministic work and specifies safe, agent-friendly script interface requirements.
checks: Explains whether scripts are justified by repetition, fragility, or determinism
Requires non-interactive input through flags, environment, files, or stdin
Requires concise --help output and useful error messages
Separates parseable stdout from diagnostics on stderr
Adds dry-run or explicit confirmation requirements for risky operations

behavior_evals.prompt: Create a reusable release-coordination skill from docs/release-runbook.md in this repository, following its packaging, validation, and eval conventions.
behavior_evals.expected_behavior: Adapts to the discovered local contract, keeps runtime and evidence separate, and makes only compatibility claims supported by tests.
checks: Discovers local validators and eval schemas before writing files
Uses the repository's existing eval shape when one exists
Runs or recommends the local validator after changes
Keeps repository-only evals outside the published runtime package
Does not assume this repository's conventions apply to unrelated targets

behavior_evals.prompt: Use skill-architect to create a Codex-only skill that requires plan mode and apply_patch.
behavior_evals.expected_behavior: Creates or proposes an honestly labeled Codex-specific integration, separating a shared runtime core only if that core remains useful on its own.
checks: Identifies plan mode and apply_patch as Codex-specific dependencies
Labels the resulting artifact or adapter as Codex-specific
Does not describe format compliance alone as cross-harness compatibility
Separates a shared core only if it has meaningful behavior without the Codex dependencies
Defines how the target integration will be validated
